text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male with known stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, MSS) with bilobar hepatic metastases, currently on maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab following 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6. Examination prompted by rising serum CEA (94.7 ng/mL, increased from 31.2 ng/mL at prior restaging approximately 8 weeks ago) and rising CA 19-9 (287 U/mL, previously 78 U/mL). Prior CT abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 8 weeks ago demonstrated stable hepatic disease. Clinical concern for disease progression.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed with administration of 120 mL of iohexol 350 intravenous contrast material. Images acquired in the portal venous phase at 70 seconds following contrast injection, with additional delayed imaging of the liver at 180 seconds. Axial images reconstructed at 3 mm intervals with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats. Lung windows reviewed for evaluation of pulmonary parenchyma. CT dose index (CTDIvol): 12.4 mGy.

COMPARISON: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (approximately 8 weeks prior); CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (end-of-induction restaging, approximately 4 months prior).

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: There are three new pulmonary nodules identified on the current examination that were definitively absent on the prior CT chest performed at the end of induction restaging and cannot be excluded on the interval CT abdomen/pelvis (chest not imaged at that time).

- Right upper lobe: A new, well-circumscribed, round soft tissue nodule measuring approximately 8 mm in greatest dimension is identified in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe (image 34, series 2). The nodule demonstrates smooth margins without associated satellite lesions or pleural tethering. No calcification or internal fat density.

- Right lower lobe: A new round nodule measuring approximately 6 mm is identified in the superior segment of the right lower lobe (image 58, series 2). Margins are smooth and well-defined. No pleural contact.

- Left upper lobe: A new soft tissue nodule measuring approximately 7 mm is identified in the apicoposterior segment of the left upper lobe (image 29, series 2). Similar morphology to the right-sided nodules. No surrounding ground-glass opacity or spiculation.

The combination of multiple new bilateral pulmonary nodules of similar morphology in the setting of a known visceral malignancy with rising tumor markers is highly concerning for pulmonary metastatic disease. The smooth margins and bilateral distribution are more characteristic of hematogenous metastases than primary bronchogenic lesions, and the appearances are not consistent with infectious or inflammatory etiology given the clinical context. Given the patient's 20 pack-year smoking history, a primary pulmonary neoplasm cannot be entirely excluded for an individual lesion, though the simultaneous development of three similar bilateral nodules makes this considerably less likely.

No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. Airways patent to subsegmental level. No evidence of central airway lesions. Mediastinal and hilar structures are normal in appearance. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes by size criteria. Heart size is within normal limits. No pericardial effusion. Aorta and great vessels are unremarkable. Bilateral axillary regions are clear.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver demonstrates expected heterogeneous enhancement on portal venous and delayed phase imaging. Previously identified hepatic lesions are re-evaluated:

- Segment VI: The previously documented hypodense, peripherally rim-enhancing hepatic lesion in segment VI has clearly enlarged on the current examination. The lesion now measures approximately 2.4 x 2.1 cm in axial dimension (image 84, series 4), representing a substantial interval increase compared with the measurement of 1.5 x 1.2 cm obtained on the prior CT performed 8 weeks ago, and compared with the post-induction measurement of 1.6 x 1.3 cm. The lesion has resumed a pattern of growth that was not present on the preceding study. Internal architecture remains consistent with necrotic metastasis with peripheral rim enhancement on delayed imaging.

- Segment II: The lesion in segment II has markedly increased in size, now measuring approximately 1.2 x 1.0 cm (image 71, series 4) compared with 0.6 x 0.5 cm on the prior examination — a doubling of its largest dimension. The lesion shows similar rim-enhancing morphology. Its proximity to the left hepatic vein is again appreciated and appears unchanged in terms of anatomic relationship, though the increased size has narrowed the apparent margin between the tumor and the vessel.

- Segment IV: The lesion in segment IV has also increased in size, now measuring approximately 1.4 x 1.1 cm (image 79, series 4) compared with 0.7 x 0.5 cm on the prior examination — representing more than a doubling from the stable measurement at the end-of-induction restaging. Again demonstrates typical metastatic rim-enhancement pattern.

- Segment VII: A tiny hypodense focus in segment VII, which had reached near-complete radiographic resolution on prior studies and was no longer measurable at the most recent prior examination, is again faintly discernible on the current study measuring approximately 5 mm on delayed imaging (image 91, series 4). Whether this represents recurrent disease or a residual scar/treated focus is uncertain; clinical correlation is recommended.

No new hepatic lesions are identified beyond those described above. No biliary ductal dilation. The portal vein and hepatic veins are patent with normal hepatopetal flow appearance. No ascites.

Peritoneum and Lymph Nodes: There is new subtle soft tissue thickening along the perihepatic region in the hepatoduodenal ligament and gastrohepatic ligament, with a small rounded lymph node measuring approximately 1.3 cm in short axis now identified in the portacaval space (image 96, series 4) — this node was not discretely identified on prior imaging and is suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement in the clinical context. An additional small node measuring 8 mm is noted in the perigastric region along the lesser curvature, which was not present on the prior examination. No peritoneal soft tissue implants or omental caking identified. No new mesenteric nodal masses.

Sigmoid Colon / Primary Site: The previously characterized sigmoid colon mass is re-evaluated. A partially obstructing soft tissue thickening/mass at the sigmoid colon is again identified, measuring approximately 3.1 x 2.5 cm in axial cross-section (image 112, series 5), increased from the prior measurement of 2.3 x 1.8 cm obtained 8 weeks ago. The mass demonstrates circumferential involvement of the sigmoid wall with associated mesenteric fat stranding. The degree of luminal compromise appears somewhat more pronounced compared with the prior examination, though the lumen remains patent. No bowel perforation. No new adjacent soft tissue extension or fistulous communication.

Spleen: Spleen is normal in size (approximately 10.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension). No splenic lesions. No splenic infarct.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously without focal mass, ductal dilation, or peripancreatic stranding. Pancreatic parenchyma is appropriate for patient age with mild parenchymal atrophy in the body. No lesions.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in size and morphology. No adrenal masses or nodules.

Kidneys: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically without hydronephrosis, focal renal mass, or stone. A 7 mm simple cyst is again noted in the upper pole of the right kidney, unchanged. No perinephric stranding.

Bladder and Pelvis: Urinary bladder is well distended and smooth-walled. No pelvic masses or lymphadenopathy. Post-sigmoid resection anatomy is not yet present (primary in situ). Rectum is unremarkable.

Bones and Soft Tissues: Visualized osseous structures demonstrate no lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for skeletal metastatic disease. No pathologic fractures. Degenerative changes are noted at the lower lumbar facet joints, consistent with age-related changes. Abdominal wall and soft tissues are unremarkable. No subcutaneous masses.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Disease progression compared with the most recent prior CT abdomen/pelvis (8 weeks ago) and the prior chest/abdomen/pelvis restaging (approximately 4 months ago). The findings are characterized by two principal components:

   a. Three new bilateral pulmonary nodules (right upper lobe 8 mm, right lower lobe 6 mm, left upper lobe 7 mm) not present on prior imaging — in the context of known metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma, rising CEA, and bilateral distribution of similar-morphology nodules, these are highly concerning for new pulmonary metastatic disease. Tissue sampling to confirm histology is advised given patient's smoking history.

   b. Clear interval growth of the previously documented hepatic metastases in segments II, IV, and VI, each demonstrating substantial size increase compared with the prior examination that showed stable measurements. Collectively, the hepatic lesion burden has increased meaningfully from the stable plateau seen during maintenance therapy.

2. New perihepatic and portacaval lymphadenopathy, most pronounced as a 1.3 cm portacaval node, consistent with new regional nodal disease.

3. Interval growth of the primary sigmoid colon mass, now measuring 3.1 x 2.5 cm, with apparent increased luminal narrowing compared with the prior study, though without frank obstruction.

4. Previously near-resolved segment VII hepatic focus is again faintly visible; significance uncertain in current context.

5. No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, osseous metastases, adrenal metastases, or pleural disease.

Overall, the imaging findings across all three anatomic compartments examined (chest, upper abdomen, and pelvis) reflect multi-site disease progression after a period of disease control on maintenance systemic therapy. The tumor marker trajectory (CEA 94.7 ng/mL, CA 19-9 287 U/mL) is concordant with these imaging findings.

Results communicated to the treating oncologist at the time of final interpretation.
